title: Sack of Constantinople by the Fourth Crusade
summary: On 13 April 1204 a Latin army raised to reconquer Jerusalem instead broke into Constantinople, the largest Christian city in the world, and gave it over to three days of pillage. The Fourth Crusade extinguished the eight-hundred-year-old East Roman capital as a Byzantine possession, carved a short-lived Latin Empire out of its territories, and inflicted a wound between the Greek and Latin churches that has never fully healed.

sections.heading: Background
sections.body:
Pope Innocent III proclaimed a new crusade in August 1198. Jerusalem had been in Muslim hands since Saladin recaptured it in 1187, and the Third Crusade of 1189 to 1192, for all its celebrity, had failed to recover it. Innocent, one of the most energetic pontiffs of the Middle Ages, wanted a fresh expedition led by lesser lords rather than kings, targeted this time at Ayyubid Egypt, which strategists believed was the true base of Muslim power in the Levant.

A group of French and Flemish nobles took the cross in 1199 and 1200 at tournaments and church councils, including Count Baldwin IX of Flanders, Count Louis of Blois, and Count Theobald III of Champagne. On Theobald's early death the marquis Boniface of Montferrat, an Italian with long-standing family ties to the Byzantine court, was chosen as overall commander. The crusaders needed ships and turned to Venice. In April 1201 their envoys, including the future chronicler Geoffrey of Villehardouin, signed the Treaty of Venice with the aged doge Enrico Dandolo, blind, in his eighties, and among the shrewdest statesmen of the age. Venice would build a fleet capable of transporting 33,500 soldiers and horses in exchange for 85,000 marks of silver and a half share of any conquests.

The crusaders miscalculated. Perhaps a third of the expected army mustered at Venice in the summer of 1202. They could not pay their debt to the Republic. Dandolo proposed a rescue operation: help Venice recover the Christian Adriatic port of Zara, which had rebelled against Venetian rule and placed itself under the King of Hungary. In November 1202 the crusaders duly assaulted and sacked Zara despite papal excommunication for making war on a Catholic city.

At Zara the exiled Byzantine prince Alexios Angelos arrived with a further offer. His father Isaac II had been deposed and blinded by his own brother Alexios III in 1195. If the crusaders would put Isaac and himself back on the throne, the young Alexios promised 200,000 silver marks, ten thousand troops for the Egyptian campaign, permanent Byzantine military support, and the reunion of the Orthodox Church with Rome. Over the objections of a minority, and against fresh papal warnings, the leadership accepted. In the summer of 1203 the fleet turned east instead of south.

sections.heading: What happened
sections.body:
The crusader fleet reached the Bosphorus on 24 June 1203. In July they broke the great chain across the Golden Horn, stormed the harbour walls with towers slung from the ships, and forced Alexios III to flee the city. The old blinded emperor Isaac II was hauled out of prison and restored, and his son crowned as co-emperor Alexios IV. Neither could deliver on the promises. The imperial treasury was empty, taxes and forced levies on churches alienated the population, and in January 1204 a court coup led by the noble Alexios Doukas, nicknamed Mourtzouphlos for his bushy joined eyebrows, murdered the young emperor. Isaac II died within days, probably of shock. Mourtzouphlos was proclaimed Alexios V.

The crusaders now had no ally inside the walls, no money, no supplies, and a mounting sense that they had been cheated. In March 1204 they signed the Treaty of Partition with Venice, agreeing to conquer the city outright and divide it. A Latin emperor would rule a quarter of the empire; three-eighths would go to the crusaders and three-eighths to Venice; the Latin Church would replace the Greek in Hagia Sophia.

A first attempt to storm the sea walls on 9 April failed with heavy losses and driving wind. On 12 April, a Monday, the wind reversed and drove the tall Venetian transports directly against the walls between the Petrion Gate and the Golden Horn. Flying bridges from the ships' fighting-tops locked onto the ramparts. A Frankish knight named Pierre d'Amiens forced his way through a postern low in the wall; a Venetian ship called the Pilgrim rammed a tower and dumped its soldiers over the parapet. By evening a wide stretch of wall was in Latin hands. Mourtzouphlos rallied troops in the central squares but the defenders melted away, and in the early hours of 13 April he fled the city.

The next morning the crusade's commanders formally allowed their men three days of sack. What followed was pillage on a scale unmatched in medieval Europe. Palaces, churches, monasteries, and private houses were stripped. The four gilded bronze horses that had crowned the Hippodrome were shipped to Venice and installed on the facade of San Marco, where their copies stand today. The Shroud of Turin's earlier history is traced by many scholars to relics seized in these days; hundreds of other relics, from a supposed head of John the Baptist to a nail of the True Cross, dispersed across Latin Europe. In Hagia Sophia a prostitute is said by the Byzantine historian Niketas Choniates, an eyewitness, to have been placed on the patriarch's throne singing bawdy songs while soldiers loaded silver and gold plate onto pack animals inside the nave. Nuns were raped; tombs of the emperors, including Justinian's, were forced open for their jewels. Fires that had already devastated three separate districts of the city between the previous summer and the final assault continued to burn.

sections.heading: Aftermath & consequences
sections.body:
In May 1204 Baldwin IX of Flanders was crowned in Hagia Sophia as Baldwin I, first Latin emperor of Constantinople. The eastern empire was formally partitioned. Venice took three-eighths of the city and a maritime empire of ports and islands stretching from the Adriatic through the Ionian and Aegean to Crete, laying the commercial foundations of its greatness for the next three centuries. Boniface of Montferrat received Thessalonica as a subordinate kingdom; French and Flemish lords carved out lordships across Greece and the islands.

A rump Byzantine world regrouped in exile. The Empire of Nicaea under Theodore Laskaris, in the Anatolian northwest, and the Empire of Trebizond on the southern shore of the Black Sea preserved Greek imperial traditions. In Epirus a further Greek principality emerged. From these bases a slow reconquest began. The Latin Empire, chronically short of manpower and money, lost ground almost from the start. In 1261 Nicaean forces under Michael VIII Palaiologos re-entered Constantinople and restored a Greek emperor to the throne, but the city they recovered was a shell: much of its population had fled, its trade quarters were dominated by Genoese and Venetians, and its imperial fabric was permanently broken.

Pope Innocent III's reaction was mixed. He excommunicated the crusaders for the assault on Zara but reversed course after Constantinople, hoping that reunion of the churches would follow. When reports of the sack reached him he wrote in horror to his legate. The formal union of the churches was never accepted by the Orthodox population, and the sack ranks with the mutual excommunications of 1054 among the events that made the East-West schism irreparable. In 2001 Pope John Paul II expressed sorrow for the actions of 1204; the Ecumenical Patriarch of Constantinople accepted the apology, but centuries of mistrust could not be erased with words.

sections.heading: Legacy
sections.body:
The Fourth Crusade never reached Egypt, let alone Jerusalem. It captured a Christian city instead of a Muslim one and did more than any Turkish army had yet done to destroy the empire that had once ruled from the Danube to the Nile. The Byzantine state that returned to Constantinople in 1261 was a regional power that could no longer hold Anatolia against the rising emirates that would become the Ottoman Turks. In this long sense the road from 1204 leads directly to 1453.

For Venice the consequences were transformative. A city of merchants became a Mediterranean empire, with colonies at Modon and Coron in the Peloponnese, in Euboea, on Crete, and in the Aegean. Venetian galleys carried spices and grain across a sea in which the Byzantine navy was no longer a rival. The bronze horses of Constantinople, still on the loggia of San Marco, are its most conspicuous trophy, but the real inheritance was the trading network.

In Latin Europe the crusade forced hard thinking. Innocent III's successors would preach further expeditions against Egypt, and eventually against heretics, pagans, and political opponents inside Europe itself; the crusading idea would never again be as clean as it had seemed in 1095. In the Orthodox world 1204 became a permanent grievance. When westerners wondered why an Orthodox archbishop in the seventeenth century preferred the sultan's turban to the cardinal's hat, they were hearing an echo of the sack. Modern historians still debate whether the diversion of the crusade was, as Villehardouin suggested, a chain of accidents or, as Niketas Choniates believed, a calculated design. Whichever it was, the consequences endured for centuries.

worldContext.text: In China the Southern Song under Emperor Ningzong were governed by the powerful chancellor Han Tuozhou, who in 1206 would launch the disastrous Kaixi War against the Jurchen Jin dynasty of the north. Steppe politics were even more consequential: the Mongol chieftain Temujin had defeated his last major rivals and would be proclaimed Genghis Khan at a great kurultai on the Onon River in 1206.

In northern India the Ghurid conquest of the Ganges plain was reaching its climax. Muhammad of Ghor's Turkic slave-general Qutb al-Din Aibak had captured Delhi in 1192 and would be recognised as sultan at Lahore in 1206, founding the Delhi Sultanate. The universities of Nalanda and Vikramashila, ancient centres of Buddhist learning, would be destroyed by his lieutenant Bakhtiyar Khilji within a few years.

In West Africa the empire of Ghana was in long decline, its trans-Saharan gold trade drifting east. The Almohad caliphate ruled the Maghreb and southern Iberia; its army would be shattered by a Christian coalition at Las Navas de Tolosa in 1212, opening the way for a rapid Reconquista in the following decades. In the Ethiopian highlands the Zagwe dynasty at Roha, later renamed Lalibela, was completing its programme of rock-cut churches.

In the American Southwest Chaco Canyon had passed its peak and its inhabitants were dispersing into the smaller pueblos of the Rio Grande and the Little Colorado. Along the Mississippi the city of Cahokia, opposite modern St Louis, was one of the largest urban centres north of Mexico, with tens of thousands of inhabitants and enormous earthen platform mounds. In the central Andes the Wari state had disintegrated and small polities filled the highlands.

Polynesian voyaging had probably reached Aotearoa New Zealand within the previous generation, initiating the Maori settlement. Rapa Nui was in the middle of the great period of moai construction. In Australia Aboriginal societies continued long-established patterns of managed burning and seasonal movement, and in Melanesia intensive agriculture supported dense highland populations.

figures.name: Pope Innocent III
figures.role: Pontiff who called the Fourth Crusade in 1198; excommunicated the crusaders for the sack of Zara and expressed horror at the sack of Constantinople

figures.name: Enrico Dandolo
figures.role: Doge of Venice, aged in his nineties and blind, who steered the crusade toward Constantinople and secured Venice's maritime empire
figures.credit: 19th-century engraving after Domenico Tintoretto, via Wikimedia Commons

figures.name: Boniface of Montferrat
figures.role: Italian marquis chosen as overall commander of the crusade in 1201; later King of Thessalonica

figures.name: Baldwin IX of Flanders
figures.role: Elected first Latin Emperor of Constantinople in May 1204; died in Bulgarian captivity within a year

figures.name: Alexios V Doukas Mourtzouphlos
figures.role: Last Byzantine emperor to defend the city; fled on 13 April 1204 and was executed by the Latins later that year

figures.name: Niketas Choniates
figures.role: Byzantine historian and eyewitness to the sack; his history is the fullest Greek account of the disaster

images.caption: Conquest of Constantinople by the Crusaders in 1204, oil on canvas by Eugene Delacroix, 1840. Musee du Louvre, Paris.
images.credit: Eugene Delacroix (1798 to 1863), via Wikimedia Commons

images.caption: Enrico Dandolo, Doge of Venice, in an idealised 19th-century engraving after a lost portrait by Domenico Tintoretto.
images.credit: After Domenico Tintoretto, 19th-century engraving, via Wikimedia Commons
